revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Buke (Adel)]][[en:Buke (Japan)]][[fa:بوکه (سامورایی)]][[fr:Buke]][[ja:武家]][[ko:무가 (일본사)]][[no:Buke]][[sv:Buke]][[zh:武家]]
{{Otheruseslist|'''Samurai Houses'''|Lineages of Japanese horse racing professionals|{{ill|Buke (family)|ja|武家 (家族)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buke (family)|1=Buke (family)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11545891}}|Households of the Chinese surname Wu|{{ill|Wu (surname 武)|en|Wu (surname 武)|qid=Q7944838|lt=Wu (surname)|1=Wu (surname 武)}}|qq=}} {{More citations needed|date=August 28, 2019|qq=}} The term '''Buke''' (武家) refers to '''Japanese families and lineages with military occupations'''. During the '''Edo period''', it specifically referred to families holding '''samurai Ranks and official court titles''' ({{ill|Samurai Court Ranks|ja|武家官位|1=Buke-kani|1=Samurai Court Ranks|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai Court Ranks|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan'i system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11545897}}). In a broader sense, the term is sometimes used to refer to the '''samurai class as a whole'''.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://dictionary.goo.ne.jp/word/%E6%AD%A6%E5%AE%B6/#jn-192066|title=武家（Buke）Meaning|publisher=Goo Japanese Dictionary|accessdate=November 5, 2020|qq=}}</ref><ref>''Kadokawa History Dictionary'' 2nd edition, Kadokawa Shoten, 1984.</ref> == Overview == During the '''mid-Heian period''', Japanese '''government offices and professional roles''' became '''hereditary''', leading to the rapid development of '''lineage-based occupations'''. Among the '''low-Ranking aristocracy''', '''some families specialized in martial arts''', forming what came to be known as '''“warrior houses”''' (兵の家). These families '''refined their military skills''' and eventually gave rise to the '''military aristocracy''' (武家貴族), the '''earliest form of the samurai class'''. These '''warrior houses''' were called '''Buke''' (武家), '''Bumon''' (武門, “martial families”), or '''Buyū no Ie''' (武勇の家, “houses of military valor”). Over time, '''powerful samurai leaders emerged''', and with the establishment of the '''Kamakura shogunate''' in '''Eastern Japan''', the '''shogunate (Bakufu) and the shogun (Kamakura-dono)''' came to be referred to as '''Buke'''. This term reflects how the '''shogunate and the Kamakura-dono (Minamoto no Yoritomo)''' pledged '''military service''' to the '''imperial court''' in exchange for '''official recognition''' of their '''policing and military powers'''. As the term '''Buke''' became firmly established, the court '''bureaucracy (monastic and civil aristocrats)''' came to be collectively known as '''Kuge (公家)''', creating a '''clear distinction between the civilian and military ruling classes'''. Among the '''military aristocracy''', the '''Seiwa Genji (清和源氏)''' and the '''Kunikiyo branch of the Kanmu Taira clan (桓武平氏国香流)''' held the '''highest noble Ranks''', typically receiving '''court titles of fourth Rank (Shii, 四位) or higher'''. Because of this, these '''two lineages''' were recognized as having the '''right to lead the samurai class''', with the '''Kawachi Genji (河内源氏)''' in particular '''monopolizing the title of Shogun'''. During the '''Sengoku period''', '''regional warlords (Daimyo)''' sought to '''enhance their status''' by '''receiving noble court Ranks''', leading to '''numerous samurai attaining high court titles (Jusanmi, 従三位, or higher)'''. With the '''rise of Tokugawa Ieyasu''', a '''systematic distinction''' between '''Buke (samurai nobility) and Kuge (imperial aristocracy)''' was established, and '''samurai court titles''' were granted only to '''Shogun, Daimyo, and Hatamoto'''. == Kamakura Period == In the early '''Kamakura period''', the '''Imperial Court''' granted '''titles and official Ranks''' to the '''shogunate, the shogun, or the Kamakura shogun’s family''', referring to them as '''Buke''' or '''Kantō (関東, “Eastern Government”)'''. However, after the '''Jōkyū War''', the '''Kamakura shogunate''' established the '''Rokuhara Tandai (六波羅探題)''' in '''Kyoto''' to oversee the imperial court. From then on, the term '''Buke''' was used '''exclusively for the Rokuhara Tandai''', while the '''shogunate itself''' was simply called '''Kantō'''. As '''high-Ranking samurai (Gokenin, 御家人)''' began receiving '''official court Ranks''', they too became recognized as '''Buke'''. Among the '''common people''', the '''shogunate and its governmental institutions''' became widely referred to as '''Buke'''. == Muromachi Period == During the '''Muromachi period''', the term '''Buke''' referred to the '''Muromachi shogunate (Bakufu), the Ashikaga shogunate, the shogun, or major Daimyo'''. == Edo Period == [[File:Samurai-Edo-Customs-1798-Shiji-no-Yukikai.png|thumb|300px|Samurai walking through town (from *Shiji no Yukikai*)]] In the '''Edo period''', '''Daimyo and high-Ranking samurai (Hatamoto)''' received '''formal noble court titles (Jūgoi-ge, 従五位下 or higher)''' and were recognized as '''Buke'''. Regardless of '''Rank''', all samurai, from '''Sotsu (足軽, foot soldiers)''' to '''Hatamoto (high retainers)''', were respectfully referred to as '''"Gobuke-sama" (御武家さま, “Honorable Samurai”)'''.<ref>It was considered highly disrespectful to call them “Osamurai-sama” (お侍さま), and doing so could result in immediate punishment under '''Kirisute Gomen''' (切捨御免, "right to strike down disrespectful commoners").</ref> The '''Tokugawa shogunate''' established the '''"Buke Shohatto" (武家諸法度, Laws for the Samurai Houses)''' to regulate the behavior of '''Buke'''. == Samurai Court Titles == In the '''Edo period''', court Ranks for '''Daimyo and below''' were considered '''"extra posts" (員外官位)''', meaning they had '''no official government role''' and were simply used as '''honorary titles for Ranking purposes''' (see: {{ill|Samurai Court Ranks|ja|武家官位|1=Buke-kani|1=Samurai Court Ranks|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Samurai Court Ranks|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan'i system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11545897}}). Samurai '''court titles''' were systematically Ranked according to the '''Daimyo’s status''', reinforcing the '''Tokugawa shogunate’s feudal hierarchy'''. == Notes == {{Reflist|qq=}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Kuge|en|Kuge|qid=Q1196276|lt=Kuge|1=Kuge}} * {{ill|Buke law|ja|武家法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buke law|1=buke law|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11545909}} * {{ill|Buke government|ja|武家政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buke government|1=samurai government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11545907}} * {{ill|Buke shohatto|en|Buke shohatto|qid=Q289817|lt=Buke Shohatto|1=Buke shohatto}} * {{ill|Buke no Tōryō|ja|武家の棟梁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buke no Tōryō|1=buke no tōryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11545892}} (Head of the Samurai Houses) * {{ill|Buke Kojitsu|ja|武家故実|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buke Kojitsu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 有職故実|1=Yūsoku Kojitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1623327}} (Samurai Traditions) * {{ill|Buke-yashiki|ja|武家屋敷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buke-yashiki|1=buke yashiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11545899}} (Samurai Residences) * {{ill|Buke Territories|ja|武家領|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buke Territories|1=buke-ryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11545912}} {{Japanese-history-stub|qq=}} {{Normdaten|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|武家|version=103366852|comment=Imported full ja history}}
